name: Illescas, Griñón, Par
name_original: Illescas, Griñón, Parla, Ciempozuelos – ruta circular
description: This moderate 50.4-mile (81.1 km) road cycling route connects Illescas, Griñón, Parla, and Ciempozuelos, offering rural and semi-urban scene
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Illescas offer varied terrain across the Spanish Central Plateau, catering to different fitness levels. The region, part of La Sagra, features mostly paved surfaces and undulating landscapes. Cyclists can explore a network of routes that provide glimpses of the surrounding countryside, despite the area's industrial presence. Best road cycling routes around Illescas The most popular road cycling route is La Sagra – circular from Illescas via Cedillo and Griñón, a 37.5 miles (60.3 km) moderate trail that takes 2 hours 33 minutes to complete. This route offers a circular journey through the La Sagra region. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Illescas, Griñón, Par, a moderate 50.4 miles (81.1 km) path. This route connects Illescas with nearby towns, offering a mix of rural and semi-urban scenery. Local road cyclists also love the Plaza de Segovia – Plaza Mayor of Navalcarnero loop from Illescas, a 48.5 miles (78.1 km) trail leading through the countryside, often completed in about 3 hours 21 minutes. Road cycling around Illescas is defined by undulating terrain, countryside views, and rural roads, with options for various ability levels. The network includes both easier and more challenging routes. The routes in Illescas are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 600 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Illescas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Illescas?
A: There are over 20 road cycling routes around Illescas, offering a variety of options for different fitness levels. You'll find routes ranging from easy to moderate difficulty.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes near Illescas?
A: Road cycling routes near Illescas feature mostly paved surfaces and undulating terrain characteristic of the Spanish Central Plateau. You can expect countryside views and rural roads, with some routes offering more significant elevation gains.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Illescas?
A: Yes, Illescas offers several road cycling routes suitable for beginners. There are 9 easy routes available, perfect for those new to road cycling or looking for a more relaxed ride.

Q: Can I find challenging road cycling routes with significant elevation gain around Illescas?
A: While most routes are moderate, some offer more challenging climbs. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39558253" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza de Segovia – Plaza Mayor of Navalcarnero loop from Illescas&lt;/a&gt; includes over 560 meters of elevation gain, providing a good workout for experienced cyclists.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available in Illescas?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Illescas are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027001795/la-sagra-circular-from-illescas-via-cedillo-and-grinon" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Sagra – circular from Illescas via Cedillo and Griñón&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20138343" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Alameda de la Sagra – Iglesia de Pantoja loop from Illescas&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there options for long-distance road cycling from Illescas?
A: Absolutely. For longer rides, consider routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e977181765/illescas-grinon-par" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Illescas, Griñón, Par&lt;/a&gt;, which spans over 81 kilometers, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39558253" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza de Segovia – Plaza Mayor of Navalcarnero loop from Illescas&lt;/a&gt;, covering over 78 kilometers.

Q: What kind of scenery will I encounter on road cycling routes near Illescas?
A: Despite some industrial areas, the cycling routes around Illescas offer pleasant countryside views, rural landscapes, and glimpses of the surrounding La Sagra region. Routes like the 'View of the Countryside loop' mentioned in regional research suggest scenic stretches.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or points of interest I can visit along road cycling routes in Illescas?
A: Yes, you can incorporate historical sites into your rides. Notable attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1393652" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Teja Spring and the Hermitage of San Isidro&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5291395" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puñoenrostro Castle&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4930785" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arched Stone Bridge in Casarrubuelos Park&lt;/a&gt;. Within Illescas itself, the Parish Church of La Asunción and the Hospital-Sanctuary of La Caridad are significant historical sites.

Q: What is the road quality like for cycling around Illescas?
A: The road cycling routes around Illescas are generally on mostly paved surfaces. While the region has an industrial presence, the routes are designed to take you through the surrounding countryside on suitable roads for cycling.

Q: Is it possible to find quiet country roads for cycling in Illescas?
A: Yes, the routes around Illescas often utilize rural roads, providing opportunities for quiet cycling away from heavy traffic. The guide highlights routes that offer countryside views and a network of less-trafficked paths.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling options in Illescas?
A: While specific 'family-friendly' designations are not provided, the presence of 9 easy routes suggests options that could be suitable for families with children who are comfortable on road bikes. These routes typically have less elevation gain and shorter distances.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Illescas?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the countryside views, and the network of rural roads that cater to different ability levels.
tips: A nice park with many different ducks. It is very neat and clean and there are fountains in case you need to refill the can.

There are five different archaeological sites in Pinto: Paleolithic, Chalcolithic, Iron Age, and Roman and Visigothic eras. In the Gonzalo Arteaga Municipal Archaeological Park you can see these discoveries. It is also worth seeing the Éboli tower, used by the Spanish kings as a prison.

Puñoenrostro Castle is an impressive medieval fortress located in Seseña (Toledo), although it is also associated with Torrejón de Velasco (Madrid) due to its proximity and recent restoration projects.
Built in the 14th century, it was granted by Henry IV to Diego Arias de Ávila, his accountant and secretary.
His descendants were named Counts of Puñoenrostro under Charles I.
In 1862, the castle was sold along with its pastureland.

faqs.question: How long does it take to complete this racebike route?
faqs.answer: This circular route, covering approximately 81 kilometers, typically takes about 3 hours and 20 minutes to complete on a racebike.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is rated as moderate. While the terrain in this region south of Madrid is generally flat with gentle undulations, it requires good fitness to complete the 81 km distance and navigate any gradual ascents.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: You can expect mostly paved surfaces, typical for a racebike route. The landscape features relatively flat sections with gentle undulations, passing through expansive agricultural fields, charming villages, and semi-urban areas. While some segments might be unpaved on other routes in the region, this route is primarily designed for road cycling.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best times to cycle this route are generally spring and autumn. During these seasons, the weather is usually mild and pleasant, offering a more comfortable experience than the summer heat or potential winter cold. The rural roads often have less heavy traffic, contributing to a tranquil ride.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes by several interesting spots. You can see the Parque del Universo, the historic Éboli Tower and Gonzalo Arteaga Municipal Archaeological Park, and the impressive Puñoenrostro Castle.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this circular route?
faqs.answer: Since this is a circular route connecting several towns, you can typically find parking in any of the towns along the route, such as Illescas, Griñón, Parla, or Ciempozuelos. Look for public parking areas within these municipalities.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or have to pay entrance fees to cycle this route. It primarily uses public roads and paths, which are freely accessible for cycling.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for cycling with dogs?
faqs.answer: As a racebike route primarily on public roads, it's not typically suitable for dogs to run alongside. If you wish to bring a dog, it would need to be safely transported in a bike trailer or similar setup. Always ensure your pet's safety and comfort.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other cycling trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, while primarily a road cycling route, it does briefly intersect with or run alongside sections of other paths. Notably, it touches parts of the MTB Madrid a Aranjuez, Ruta Verde 2.2 Canal del Jarama, and the Vereda Toledana.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect while cycling?
faqs.answer: The route offers a diverse blend of rural and semi-urban scenery. You'll cycle through extensive agricultural fields, including cereal crops, olive groves, and vineyards, interspersed with charming Spanish villages. Depending on the specific roads, you might also catch glimpses of natural areas like the Southeast Regional Park near Ciempozuelos.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for refreshments or food along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route connects the towns of Illescas, Griñón, Parla, and Ciempozuelos, you will find various cafes, restaurants, and shops in these municipalities where you can stop for refreshments, food, or to refill water bottles.
